terms.definition: garments, clothes; material bodies as temporary coverings for the eternal soul; the changing vehicles that the soul inhabits like worn garments being replaced
worn out, old, decayed; the material body after its utility is exhausted and must be relinquished; the natural state of all material forms through time
having abandoned, given up; voluntary relinquishment of the old body at death; the conscious release of worn-out form without attachment or regret
the embodied one, the soul; literally 'possessing a body'; the conscious entity distinct from the body itself; the wearer of garments versus the garments themselves
verily accepts, enters into; the soul's active assumption of a new material body according to accumulated karma; the taking up of fresh garments
terms.roots: from sam-yā (to go toward, attain, accept)

terms.definition: Equally disposed, same, impartial, level. The Lord's equal disposition toward all beings—no differentiation or preference. Not emotional neutrality but ontological equality.
In all living entities. Sara (all) + bhūta (living being, entity). The comprehensive scope of the Lord's equal presence—no being is excluded from divine presence.
Hateful, odious, one who is to be hated. From dviṣ (to hate). The Lord declares no one is hateful to Him—hatred finds no target in the Absolute.
They worship, serve, share in, enjoy. From bhaj (to share, serve). The verb of loving devotion—active engagement with the Divine. Not contemplation but participatory connection.
By devotion, with devotion. The instrumental means of connection—devotion is not the goal but the tool, the method, the instrument that opens the heart to divine reciprocation.
prescribed, regulated, bound by cosmic/dharmic order; duty not chosen but inherited through circumstance and station
action, work, ritual performance; specifically niyata-karma: duty performed without attachment to result
superior, better, more excellent; comparative of jya (to overpower) — the moral hierarchy between performed duty and renunciatory evasion
non-action, inaction, renunciation without qualification; the false renunciation of the immature practitioner
bodily maintenance, journey of the body (sharira: that which decays + yatra: going) — the minimum requirement of prakriti that cannot be evaded
would be accomplished, effected, made successful; from pra-sidh (to execute/complete)
The five organs of action (hands, legs, speech, genitals, anus) having been controlled/restrained. The external show of physical restraint that defines the pretender.

terms.type: verb, present middle voice 3rd person singular
terms.definition: Sits, remains, dwells. From ās (to sit). The pretender's posture of meditation—body still while mind wanders. The middle voice emphasizes self-benefit that never arrives.
Thinking of, dwelling upon, remembering. From smṛ (to remember). The smoking gun of the pretender—the mind actively engaging with sense objects while body feigns renunciation.
Objects of the senses. Indriya (sense) + artha (object/purpose) + ān (accusative plural). The viṣaya that the pretender's mind secretly craves while body remains still.
One whose self is deluded/bewildered. Vimūḍha (deluded) + ātmā (self). Not merely confused but thoroughly deluded—vi (intensive) + mūḍha. The pretender's true state.
